file.content:
# MHPCO Claim Office Kata

## Setting

You are building the policy management system for the **Most Honorable
Privileged Claims Office for Magical Risks and Cursed Items** (MHPCO),
an old-fashioned insurance institution that covers magical items —
swords, amulets, staves, potions, and components such as runes and
moonstones. The MHPCO is bureaucratic, slightly stingy, and proud of
its venerable traditions.

## Feature

The system supports two operations: **quote** computes the premium for
a list of items a customer wishes to insure, and **claim** processes a
damage report against an existing policy.

## Rules

### Item values and base premiums

Each main item has an insurance value and a base premium according to
the MHPCO price list:

- Sword: 1000 G insurance value, 100 G base premium
- Amulet: 600 G / 60 G
- Staff: 800 G / 80 G
- Potion: 400 G / 40 G

Components — for example runes and moonstones — are insured at 250 G
each, with a base premium of 25 G per component. A building block of
3 alike components is offered at a special base premium of 60 G.

### Premium modifiers

- Cursed items add a 50 % risk surcharge.
- Highly enchanted items (enchantment level ≥ 5) add a 30 % risk
  surcharge.
- Long-standing customers (≥ 2 years of business with MHPCO) receive a
  20 % loyalty discount.
- A first insurance carries a 10 % initial assessment surcharge.
- Customers receive a 15 % discount on each contract after their first.
- A 5 G processing fee is added to every premium.

All amounts are rounded to whole G in the MHPCO's favor.

### Claim processing

A deductible of 100 G applies per damage event. The total payout per
policy is capped at twice the insurance sum.

- Damage to items with enchantment level ≥ 8 is reimbursed at 50 % of
  the damage amount.
- Damage to items made of dragon material is fully reimbursed.

## Examples

The examples below illustrate single rules in isolation. Values shown
are base premiums or raw payout amounts before integration with other
modifiers, unless stated otherwise.

### Building block of 3 alike components

- 2 runes → 50 G base premium
- 3 runes → 60 G base premium (block applies)
- 4 runes → 100 G base premium (no block — block requires exactly 3)
- 7 runes → 175 G base premium

### "Alike" components

> ❓ Hmm — "alike" components: does that mean exactly the same type,
> or just the same family (rune-y, gemstone-y)?

- 2 runes + 1 moonstone → 75 G base premium (no block: different types)
- 3 runes + 3 moonstones → 120 G base premium (two separate blocks)

### Modifier scope on multi-item policies

> ❓ A policy with several items: does the cursed surcharge apply to
> the whole policy, or just to the cursed item?

- a policy covers a cursed sword (base premium 100 G) and a plain
  amulet (base premium 60 G) → policy base premium 160 G; the cursed
  surcharge adds 50 G (50 % of the cursed sword's base premium, not
  of the policy total) → 210 G before further modifiers and fee
- item-specific modifiers (cursed, high enchantment) apply to the
  base premium of the affected item; policy-wide modifiers (loyalty,
  first insurance, follow-up contract) apply to the policy base
  premium (the sum of all item base premiums); the processing fee is
  added at the very end

### Modifier thresholds

- customer with exactly 2 years with MHPCO → loyalty discount applies
- sword with exactly enchantment 5 → high-enchantment surcharge
  applies; if cursed, both surcharges apply
- sword with enchantment 4 → no high-enchantment surcharge; curse
  surcharge applies only if cursed
- dragon-material sword with exactly enchantment 8, damage 1000 G →
  payout 400 G (high-enchantment clause applies, then deductible)

### Deductible per damage event

- a dragon attack damages an insured sword (500 G) and an insured
  amulet (300 G); payout = 600 G (the 100 G deductible applies once
  per damaged item)

### Standard reimbursement (no special clauses)

- regular sword (steel, enchantment 3), damage 500 G → payout 400 G
  (full reimbursement minus 100 G deductible; no special clause applies)
- damage to a rune (insurance value 250 G), damage 200 G → payout
  100 G (full reimbursement minus 100 G deductible; runes have no
  enchantment level or material, so no special clause applies)

### Enchantment threshold vs. dragon material

- dragon-material sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G → payout 400 G
  (both clauses apply; the 50 % rule wins, then deductible: 500 − 100)
- dragon-material sword, enchantment 5, damage 800 G → payout 700 G
  (only the dragon-material clause applies: full reimbursement,
  then deductible: 800 − 100)
- steel sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G → payout 400 G
  (only the high-enchantment clause applies: 50 % first, then
  deductible: 500 − 100)

### Multiple items of the same type

> ❓ What if a customer brings two of the same item — one in hand,
> one in the backpack? Or just two swords because they fight with
> two?

- a policy covers two swords → insurance sum 2000 G (= 2×1000),
  cap 4000 G
- a dragon attack damages both swords; `damages` contains two
  `{itemType: "sword", ...}` entries → each entry is treated as a
  separate damage with its own deductible
- if the `damages` array contains more entries of a given type than
  the policy actually covers (e.g. two sword damages but only one
  sword insured) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code; the
  whole claim is rejected

### Cap exhaustion

- a policy covers a sword and an amulet → insurance sum 1600 G
  (= 1000 + 600, the sum of the items' insurance values), cap 3200 G
- a cursed sword (insurance value 1000 G, premium with modifiers
  165 G) → cap 2000 G (based on the unmodified insurance value;
  premium modifiers do not raise the cap)
- a policy covers a sword and 3 runes (a block) → insurance sum
  1750 G (= 1000 + 3×250); the block discount affects the premium
  only, not the insurance sum
- a sword is insured (insurance sum 1000 G, cap 2000 G); two successive
  claims of 1500 G each
- first claim → payout 1400 G, cap remaining 600 G
- second claim → payout 600 G, cap remaining 0 G (the desired 1400 G
  is reduced to the remaining cap)

### Rounding in the MHPCO's favor

- a premium calculation that yields 197.5 G → final premium 198 G
  (rounded up)
- a payout calculation that yields 350.5 G → final payout 350 G
  (rounded down)
- intermediate amounts during a calculation are kept as fractions;
  only the final premium or payout is rounded

### Edge cases

- empty item list → premium 5 G (only the processing fee)
- quote includes an item with an unknown type (e.g.
  `{type: "broomstick"}`) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code
  and writes an error description to stderr; no `results` are written
  to stdout
- claim references a damage entry whose item is not part of the policy
  (e.g. an amulet damaged when only a sword is insured, or an item
  with an unknown type) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code
  and writes an error description to stderr
- claim contains a damage entry with `amount: -200` → the CLI exits
  with a non-zero status code and writes an error description to
  stderr

## Integration examples

The following two examples combine multiple rules to clarify how
modifiers stack and how customer history affects the premium.

### Newcomer with a cursed sword

- customer: 0 years with MHPCO, no previous contract
- item: a cursed sword (steel, enchantment 3)
- premium: 165 G
- (100 G base + 50 G curse + 10 G first insurance = 160 G + 5 G fee = 165 G)

### Long-standing customer's second contract

> ❓ Wait — does "first insurance" mean the customer's first ever
> contract, or the first time we see this particular item? A
> long-standing customer with a brand-new sword: does the first
> insurance surcharge still apply?

- customer: 3 years with MHPCO; this is the customer's second `quote`
  in the scenario
- item: a cursed sword (steel, enchantment 7)
- premium: 160 G
- (100 G base + 50 G curse + 30 G high enchantment − 20 G loyalty
  + 10 G first insurance − 15 G follow-up contract = 155 G + 5 G fee
  = 160 G)
- The first insurance surcharge still applies to the new sword, even
  though the customer is on a follow-up contract — each item in a
  `quote` is treated as a first insurance, regardless of customer
  history.

## CLI input/output format

The implementation must be exposed as a command-line executable
`claim-office`. It reads a JSON document from stdin describing a
scenario and writes a JSON document with the corresponding results to
stdout.

A scenario is a JSON object with a `customer` object and a `steps`
array. The `customer` describes the single customer for the whole
scenario. Each step has an `op` field (`"quote"` or `"claim"`) and the
operation-specific input fields. Steps are processed sequentially;
later `claim` steps refer to a policy created by an earlier `quote`
step via its zero-based step index in a `policy` field.

Stdout receives a JSON object with a `results` array of the same length
and order as the input `steps`. Each result contains:

- For a `quote` step: `premium` (integer, total premium in G).
- For a `claim` step: `payout` (integer, payout in G) and
  `remainingCap` (integer, cap remaining on the policy after this
  claim).

### Schema example

Stdin:

```json
{
  "customer": {"yearsWithMHPCO": 5},
  "steps": [
    {
      "op": "quote",
      "items": [
        {"type": "amulet", "material": "silver", "enchantment": 2, "cursed": false}
      ]
    },
    {
      "op": "claim",
      "policy": 0,
      "incident": {
        "cause": "fire",
        "damages": [
          {"itemType": "amulet", "amount": 200}
        ]
      }
    }
  ]
}
```

Stdout shape:

```json
{"results": [{"premium": <integer>}, {"payout": <integer>, "remainingCap": <integer>}]}
```

## Expected Output Files

- Source files implementing the logic and the `claim-office` CLI
- Test files
- CLI entry point at `src/cli.ts` (reads JSON from stdin, writes JSON to stdout)

## Constraints

- Use TypeScript for the implementation
- The CLI must read JSON from stdin and write JSON to stdout
- The CLI entry point must be at `src/cli.ts`
- Internal data structures and module organization are free

- ❌ Prediction wrong → STOP and explain discrepancy

### Step 4: Create Empty Function

Create minimal function stub (no logic):

```typescript
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  return undefined as unknown as number;
};
```

### Step 5: Predict Runtime Error

Before running again, state your prediction:

```
🔴 Red Phase - Runtime Error Prediction:
- Test: "should return 0 for empty input"
- Expected: Runtime assertion error
- Expected value: 0
- Actual value: undefined
- Diff:
  Expected: 0
  Received: undefined
```

### Step 6: Run Test - Verify Runtime Error

Run `pnpm test:unit:basic` and verify:
- ✅ Assertion error as predicted, OR
- ❌ Prediction wrong → STOP and explain discrepancy

### Step 7: Report Completion

You MUST output the full Step 7 block verbatim with `Correct` or `Incorrect`
chosen for each prediction. Do not abbreviate. Do not collapse the two
prediction lines into one.

```
🔴 Red Phase Complete:
**Test Activated**: "should return 0 for empty input"
**Compilation Prediction**: Cannot find name 'calculate' ✅ Correct
**Runtime Prediction**: Expected 0, received undefined ✅ Correct
**Result**: Test fails as expected with assertion error

Proceeding to Green phase.
```

## Important Guidelines

### DO
- ✅ Activate exactly ONE test at a time
- ✅ Make explicit predictions before running tests
- ✅ Verify test fails for the right reason
- ✅ Keep all other tests as `it.todo()`

### DON'T
- ❌ Activate multiple tests
- ❌ Skip making predictions
- ❌ Write implementation to make test pass
- ❌ Continue if prediction fails without explanation

skills.name: green
skills.content:
# TDD Green Phase

You are now in the **Green Phase** of TDD. Follow these instructions to make the failing test pass with MINIMAL code.

## Your Mission

1. Implement the minimal code necessary to make the failing test pass
2. Use the simplest possible solution (hardcoded values are acceptable)
3. Do not add features for future tests
4. Verify all tests pass
5. No optimization, no refactoring during Green phase

## Context: Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Failing test: "claim with no damage on sword policy: payout 0, cap remains 2000"
Current error: claim not handled. Need to: process claim step, compute payout (sum per damage minus deductible), compute remainingCap (= 2*insuranceSum - cumulativePayout).
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts

Cap = 2*insurance sum (sword: 1000 → cap 2000). For empty damages, payout 0 and remainingCap 2000.

## Green Phase Rules

- **Minimal code only**: Just enough to pass the current test
- **Smallest change**: Make the smallest possible change
- **No future features**: Don't implement what future tests might need
- **Hardcoded returns are allowed**
- **Tests must pass**: Verify all tests are green
- **No refactoring during Green phase**: Save improvements for Refactor phase

## Process

### Step 1: Analyze the Failing Test

Identify:
- What input does the test provide?
- What output does it expect?
- What is the simplest way to produce that output?

### Step 2: Write Minimal Implementation

Implement only what's needed to make the current test pass:

```typescript
// For first test "should return 0 for empty input":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  return 0;
};

// For second test "should return number for single input":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  if (input === "") return 0;
  return parseInt(input);
};

// For third test "should add two numbers":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  if (input === "") return 0;
  const numbers = input.split(",");
  if (numbers.length === 1) return parseInt(numbers[0]);
  return parseInt(numbers[0]) + parseInt(numbers[1]);
};
```

### Step 3: Run Tests

Run `pnpm test:unit:basic` and verify:
- Current test now passes ✅
- All previous tests still pass ✅

### Step 4: Verify No Over-Implementation

Check:
- ❓ Did I implement features for future tests? → Remove them
- ❓ Did I add logic not demanded by current test? → Remove it
- ❓ Did I optimize prematurely? → Simplify
- ❓ Did I refactor existing code? → Revert, save for Refactor phase

### Step 5: Report Completion

```
🟢 Green Phase Complete:
**Implementation**: [brief description of what was added]
**Result**: All tests now pass ([X] passing)

Proceeding to Refactor phase.
```

## Minimal Implementation Strategies

### Hardcoded Returns

```typescript
// Test: "should return 0 for empty input"
return 0;
```

### Simple Conditionals

```typescript
// Test: "should return number for single input"
if (input === "") return 0;
return parseInt(input);
```

### Generalization (only when forced by a new test)

```typescript
// Test: "should add multiple numbers"
return input.split(",").reduce((sum, n) => sum + parseInt(n), 0);
```

## Important Guidelines

### DO
- ✅ Write minimal code to make test pass
- ✅ Use hardcoded values when appropriate
- ✅ Take small steps
- ✅ Verify all tests pass

### DON'T
- ❌ Implement beyond what tests demand
- ❌ Add features for future tests
- ❌ Optimize prematurely
- ❌ Refactor during Green phase

## Completion

After completing Green phase, proceed to Refactor phase:

```
🟢 Green Phase Complete. Proceeding to Refactor phase.
```

skills.name: test-list
skills.content:
# TDD Test List Phase

You are now in the **Test List Phase** of TDD. Follow these instructions to create a comprehensive test list.

## Your Mission

Create a test list using `it.todo()` for BASE FUNCTIONALITY ONLY:
1. Identify the **core/base functionality** of the feature
2. Break it down into discrete, testable behaviors
3. Create test cases using `it.todo()` - NO executable tests yet
4. Order tests from simplest to most complex
5. Avoid advanced features or edge cases

## Context: Feature: MHPCO Claim Office - quote and claim operations.
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Requirements: See prompt.md. Need to support:
- quote: computes premium for items (sword/amulet/staff/potion, components like runes/moonstones with block-of-3 discount)
- claim: processes damage with deductible, cap, high-enchantment/dragon material rules
- Premium modifiers: cursed +50%, high enchantment (>=5) +30%, loyalty (>=2 years) -20%, first insurance +10%, follow-up contract -15%, +5G fee
- Rounding: premium up, payout down (in MHPCO favor)
- CLI at src/cli.ts reading JSON stdin, writing JSON stdout
- Scenario with customer + sequential steps (quote/claim), claim refs policy by step index
- Error cases: unknown item type, damage to non-policy item, negative damage amount, more damage entries than insured items

## Test List Rules

### What to Include (Base Functionality)
- **Empty/zero cases**: What happens with empty input?
- **Single element cases**: Simplest non-empty input
- **Two element cases**: Introduces interaction
- **Multiple element cases**: Generalizes the pattern
- **Basic validation**: Essential constraints only

### What to Exclude (Save for Later)
- Advanced features
- Edge cases
- Performance optimizations
- Exotic inputs
- Error handling beyond basics

## Process

### Step 1: Understand the Feature
- What is the core functionality?
- What are the **essential behaviors** (not nice-to-haves)?
- What is the **minimum viable feature**?

### Step 2: Identify Base Test Cases
List 3-6 test cases covering base functionality only.

### Step 3: Order Tests (Simple → Complex)
1. Simplest case (often empty/zero)
2. Single element
3. Two elements
4. Multiple elements
5. Basic validation

### Step 4: Write Test File
Create the test file with `it.todo()` entries:

```typescript
import { describe, it, expect } from "vitest";
import { functionName } from "./implementation.js";

describe("Feature Name", () => {
  it.todo("should [simplest case]");
  it.todo("should [next case]");
  it.todo("should [more complex case]");
  // ... ordered simple → complex
});
```

### Step 5: Provide Summary

After creating the test list, output:

```
📋 Test List Created:
**Feature**: [feature name]
**Test File**: [filename].spec.ts
**Base Functionality Tests**: [count]

**Test Cases** (ordered simple → complex):
1. ✅ [first test description]
2. ✅ [second test description]
3. ✅ [third test description]
...

**Advanced Features** (NOT included):
- [feature 1] - save for later
- [feature 2] - save for later

**Next Step**: Invoke `/red` to activate the first test.
```

## Important Guidelines

### DO
- ✅ Focus on **base functionality only**
- ✅ Order tests **simple → complex**
- ✅ Use `it.todo()` for all tests
- ✅ Write **clear, specific descriptions**
- ✅ Keep tests **independent**
- ✅ One behavior per test

### DON'T
- ❌ Include advanced features
- ❌ Write executable tests (use `it.todo()`)
- ❌ Think about implementation
- ❌ Include edge cases
- ❌ Order randomly

## Completion

After completing the test list, proceed to Red phase:

```
📋 Test List Phase Complete. Proceeding to Red phase with the first test.
```

content.text:
Refactor Phase Complete.

## Summary

### Naming Evaluation (First Priority)
Reviewed all function names: `runScenario`, `computeQuote`, `claimStep`, `createPolicy`, `policyBasePremium`, `componentGroupPremium`, `itemModifierSurcharge`, `policyModifierAmount`, etc. All clearly reveal intent. **No renaming needed.**

### Refactoring Applied: Extract Step Handlers + Encapsulate Scenario State

**Before** — `runScenario` mixed three concerns: state setup, step routing, and quote/claim logic inline. It used a separate `let quoteCount = 0` plus `policies` array as loose state with mutations sprinkled across the map callback.

**After** — Introduced a `ScenarioState` interface bundling `policies`, `quoteCount`, and `yearsWithMHPCO`, plus two handler functions:
- `handleQuoteStep(step, state)` — owns quote-related state mutations and context construction
- `handleClaimStep(step, state)` — resolves the policy and delegates to `claimStep`

`runScenario` now reads as: build state, dispatch each step to its handler, return results.

### Simple Design Rules Applied
- **Rule 1 (Tests Pass)**: All 21 tests still green
- **Rule 2 (Reveals Intent)**: Top-level orchestrator is now a 3-line dispatch — quote vs. claim handling is named explicitly rather than inlined. Loose `let quoteCount` is gone; state is a coherent object.
- **Rule 3 (No Duplication)**: No duplication introduced; scenario state assembly is centralized
- **Rule 4 (Fewest Elements)**: Added one interface and two small functions, but they replace inline logic of similar size while improving cohesion

### APP Mass Approximation

**Before** `runScenario`: ~50 (1 conditional, 1 loop, multiple assignments to `quoteCount`, inline ctx object literal)

**After** total across `runScenario` + `handleQuoteStep` + `handleClaimStep`: similar overall mass (~52), but:
- `runScenario` itself dropped from ~50 → ~20 (one ternary in a map)
- Each handler is small and focused (~15-20 each)
- Mutation surface is constrained to `handleQuoteStep`

This is a case where **Rule 2 (clarity) takes priority over raw mass**. The overall mass is roughly equivalent, but readability and locality of mutation are substantially improved.

### Files
- `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-18_19-55-22_claim-office-example-mapping_v6.3-no-pep_opus-4-7-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts` — refactored `runScenario` and added `ScenarioState`, `handleQuoteStep`, `handleClaimStep`

### Tests
All 21 passing, 12 todo remaining.

# TDD Red Phase

You are now in the **Red Phase** of TDD. Follow these instructions to activate ONE test and make it fail.

## Your Mission

1. Activate exactly ONE test from the test list
2. Make explicit predictions about how it will fail
3. Verify the test fails for the right reason
4. NO implementation during Red phase

## Context: Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Activate: "claim on rune (no enchantment/material): damage 200 → payout 100"
Current passing tests: 21
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts

## Red Phase Rules

- **One test at a time**: Convert exactly ONE `it.todo()` to executable test
- **All other tests remain as `it.todo()`**: Never have more than one failing test
- **Two-stage failure**: First compilation error, then runtime/assertion error
- **Make predictions**: Explicitly state expected failures before running tests
- **No implementation**: Don't write code to make test pass yet

## Process

### Step 1: Activate One Test

Identify the next `it.todo()` and convert it to executable test code:

```typescript
// Convert from:
it.todo("should return 0 for empty input");

// To:
it("should return 0 for empty input", () => {
  expect(calculate("")).toBe(0);
});
```

Leave all other tests as `it.todo()`.

Create minimal function stub (no logic):

```typescript
};
```

### Step 5: Predict Runtime Error

Before running again, state your prediction:

```
🔴 Red Phase - Runtime Error Prediction:
- Test: "should return 0 for empty input"

```

### Step 6: Run Test - Verify Runtime Error

### Step 7: Report Completion


## Important Guidelines

### DO
- ✅ Activate exactly ONE test at a time

### DON'T

```
Investigating the discrepancy before proceeding.
```

content.text:
All 22 tests still pass.

**Refactoring Summary**:

**Refactoring Applied**:

1. **Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) - Naming**: Renamed `claimStep` (function) to `processClaim`. The old name collided with the `ClaimStep` type/interface, creating ambiguity. The new name clearly indicates an action (verb) distinct from the data shape (`ClaimStep`).

2. **Rule 3 (No Duplication) / Rule 2 (Reveals Intent)**: Extracted the reduce expression `step.incident.damages.reduce((sum, d) => sum + damagePayout(d), 0)` into a named helper `sumDamagePayouts(damages)`. This matches the pattern of existing helpers (`sumMainItemPremiums`, `sumComponentPremiums`, `sumItemModifiers`, `insuranceSumOf`) and reveals intent at the call site.

**APP Mass Analysis**:

Before — `claimStep` body:
- Constants: 1 (`0`) = 1
- Bindings: ~5 (sum, d, desired, payout) = 5
- Invocations: 5 (reduce, +, damagePayout, Math.min, Math.floor) = 10
- Assignments: 1 (-=) = 6
- Total: ~22

After — `processClaim` + `sumDamagePayouts`:
- `sumDamagePayouts`: 1 constant + 3 bindings + 3 invocations = 1 + 3 + 6 = 10
- `processClaim`: 0 constants + 3 bindings + 3 invocations + 1 assignment = 0 + 3 + 6 + 6 = 15
- Combined: ~25

Mass change: **22 → 25 (Δ +3)**. APP mass increased slightly due to function extraction overhead. **However, Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) trumps APP**: the extraction matches the existing codebase's helper naming pattern and the rename eliminates name collision with the `ClaimStep` type.

**Tests**: All 22 tests passing.

- /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-18_19-55-22_claim-office-example-mapping_v6.3-no-pep_opus-4-7-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts

content.text:
# TDD Red Phase

You are now in the **Red Phase** of TDD. Follow these instructions to activate ONE test and make it fail.

## Your Mission

1. Activate exactly ONE test from the test list
2. Make explicit predictions about how it will fail
3. Verify the test fails for the right reason
4. NO implementation during Red phase

## Context: Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Activate: "dragon-material sword fully reimbursed minus deductible"
Current passing tests: 22
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts

## Red Phase Rules

- **One test at a time**: Convert exactly ONE `it.todo()` to executable test
- **All other tests remain as `it.todo()`**: Never have more than one failing test
- **Two-stage failure**: First compilation error, then runtime/assertion error
- **Make predictions**: Explicitly state expected failures before running tests
- **No implementation**: Don't write code to make test pass yet

## Process

### Step 1: Activate One Test

Identify the next `it.todo()` and convert it to executable test code:

```typescript
// Convert from:
it.todo("should return 0 for empty input");

// To:
it("should return 0 for empty input", () => {
  expect(calculate("")).toBe(0);
});
```

Leave all other tests as `it.todo()`.

### Step 2: Predict Compilation Error

Before running the test, state your prediction:

```
🔴 Red Phase - Compilation Error Prediction:
- Test: "should return 0 for empty input"
- Expected: Compilation error
- Reason: Function `calculate` doesn't exist yet
- Error: "Cannot find name 'calculate'"
```

### Step 3: Run Test - Verify Compilation Error

Run `pnpm test:unit:basic` and verify:
- ✅ Compilation error as predicted, OR
- ❌ Prediction wrong → STOP and explain discrepancy

### Step 4: Create Empty Function

Create minimal function stub (no logic):

```typescript
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  return undefined as unknown as number;
};
```

### Step 5: Predict Runtime Error

Before running again, state your prediction:

```
🔴 Red Phase - Runtime Error Prediction:
- Test: "should return 0 for empty input"
- Expected: Runtime assertion error
- Expected value: 0
- Actual value: undefined
- Diff:
  Expected: 0
  Received: undefined
```

### Step 6: Run Test - Verify Runtime Error

Run `pnpm test:unit:basic` and verify:
- ✅ Assertion error as predicted, OR
- ❌ Prediction wrong → STOP and explain discrepancy

### Step 7: Report Completion

You MUST output the full Step 7 block verbatim with `Correct` or `Incorrect`
chosen for each prediction. Do not abbreviate. Do not collapse the two
prediction lines into one.

```
🔴 Red Phase Complete:
**Test Activated**: "should return 0 for empty input"
**Compilation Prediction**: Cannot find name 'calculate' ✅ Correct
**Runtime Prediction**: Expected 0, received undefined ✅ Correct
**Result**: Test fails as expected with assertion error

Proceeding to Green phase.
```

## Important Guidelines

### DO
- ✅ Activate exactly ONE test at a time
- ✅ Make explicit predictions before running tests
- ✅ Verify test fails for the right reason
- ✅ Keep all other tests as `it.todo()`

### DON'T
- ❌ Activate multiple tests
- ❌ Skip making predictions
- ❌ Write implementation to make test pass
- ❌ Continue if prediction fails without explanation

## Prediction Failure Protocol

If your prediction was wrong:

```
❌ Prediction Failed:
- Predicted: [what you expected]
- Actual: [what happened]
- Discrepancy: [explanation]

Investigating the discrepancy before proceeding.
```

## Completion

After completing Red phase, proceed to Green phase:

```
🔴 Red Phase Complete. Proceeding to Green phase.
```

This test introduces dragon-material handling. Per the spec, for a standard (non-dragon, low-enchantment) item, payout = damage - deductible. For dragon-material, the spec says "fully reimbursed" then deductible — which equals damage - deductible for damage ≤ insurance value. So this test alone may pass with existing code. Let me pick a damage value that exposes the difference: per "Cap exhaustion" example, dragon material is "fully reimbursed" — meaning the insurance-value cap doesn't apply to the per-damage payout in the same way. Actually re-reading: the standard reimbursement says full minus deductible already. So this single test may not force a new branch. I'll pick damage = 1500 to test that dragon material allows the full damage to be reimbursed (without per-item cap), then 100 deductible.

Actually looking again at the spec carefully:
- Standard (no special clauses): "full reimbursement minus 100 G deductible" for damage 500. So default IS full reimbursement minus deductible.
- High enchantment ≥8: 50% then deductible.
- Dragon material: "fully reimbursed" — same as standard.

So "dragon-material sword" without high enchantment behaves exactly like standard. The test will pass with current code.

content.text:
All 23 tests pass.

**Refactoring Summary**

**Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)**:
- `damagePayout`: Name clearly describes purpose — kept.
- `sumDamagePayouts`: Clear — kept.
- `processClaim`: Clear — kept.
- Inside `processClaim`, the local variable `desired` was ambiguous about what it represented. Renamed for clarity.

**Refactoring Applied**:
- Rule 2 (Reveals Intent): Renamed `desired` → `uncappedPayout` in `processClaim`. The variable represents the sum of damage payouts BEFORE the cap is applied (then `payout = min(uncappedPayout, remainingCap)`). The new name explicitly states this is the pre-cap amount, making the cap application logic more self-documenting.

**APP Mass Analysis**:

Before (`processClaim`):
- bindings: 4 (step, policy, desired, payout) = 4
- invocations: 3 (sumDamagePayouts, Math.min, Math.floor) = 6
- assignments: 1 (-=) = 6
- property accesses (counted as bindings): minimal
- Total relevant mass: ~16

After (`processClaim`):
- bindings: 4 (step, policy, uncappedPayout, payout) = 4
- invocations: 3 = 6
- assignments: 1 = 6
- Total relevant mass: ~16

**Mass Change**: 16 → 16 (Δ 0) — unchanged.

**Rationale**: Mass is identical (a rename doesn't change structure), but clarity is improved. Per Simple Design Rule 2 (Reveals Intent), this is a valid refactoring even when mass is unchanged. Rule 2 trumps APP.

**Other Rules Evaluated**:
- Rule 1 (Tests Pass): All 23 tests green.
- Rule 3 (No Duplication): No duplication found across the implementation. The premium calculation, claim handling, and state management are well-separated.
- Rule 4 (Fewest Elements): The code uses small, well-named helper functions. Each abstraction (`isComponent`, `componentGroupPremium`, `policyModifierAmount`, etc.) serves a clear purpose. No unnecessary elements.

**Other Considerations**:
- The mutation in `processClaim` (`policy.remainingCap -= payout`) could be eliminated by returning a new policy state. However, this would require restructuring how `state.policies` is updated, increasing complexity elsewhere. The current localized mutation is acceptable.
- The `Math.floor` is applied after `Math.min(uncappedPayout, remainingCap)`. Both `uncappedPayout` and `remainingCap` are integers in current tests, so floor is a no-op now but is correct per the spec ("payout rounds down in MHPCO's favor"). This is a defensive correctness measure for the upcoming `payout rounds down` test.

**Files modified**:
- `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-18_19-55-22_claim-office-example-mapping_v6.3-no-pep_opus-4-7-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts` (renamed `desired` → `uncappedPayout` in `processClaim`)

Refactor phase complete. All 23 tests passing.

- **Hardcoded returns are allowed**
- **Tests must pass**: Verify all tests are green
- **No refactoring during Green phase**: Save improvements for Refactor phase

## Process

### Step 1: Analyze the Failing Test

Identify:
- What input does the test provide?
- What output does it expect?
- What is the simplest way to produce that output?

### Step 2: Write Minimal Implementation

Implement only what's needed to make the current test pass:

```typescript
// For first test "should return 0 for empty input":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  return 0;
};

// For second test "should return number for single input":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  if (input === "") return 0;
  return parseInt(input);
};

// For third test "should add two numbers":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  if (input === "") return 0;
  const numbers = input.split(",");
  if (numbers.length === 1) return parseInt(numbers[0]);
  return parseInt(numbers[0]) + parseInt(numbers[1]);
};
```

### Step 3: Run Tests

Run `pnpm test:unit:basic` and verify:
- Current test now passes ✅
- All previous tests still pass ✅

### Step 4: Verify No Over-Implementation

Check:
- ❓ Did I implement features for future tests? → Remove them
- ❓ Did I add logic not demanded by current test? → Remove it
- ❓ Did I optimize prematurely? → Simplify
- ❓ Did I refactor existing code? → Revert, save for Refactor phase

### Step 5: Report Completion

```
🟢 Green Phase Complete:
**Implementation**: [brief description of what was added]
**Result**: All tests now pass ([X] passing)

Proceeding to Refactor phase.
```

## Minimal Implementation Strategies

### Hardcoded Returns

```typescript
// Test: "should return 0 for empty input"
return 0;
```

### Simple Conditionals

```typescript
// Test: "should return number for single input"
if (input === "") return 0;
return parseInt(input);
```

### Generalization (only when forced by a new test)

```typescript
// Test: "should add multiple numbers"
return input.split(",").reduce((sum, n) => sum + parseInt(n), 0);
```

## Important Guidelines

### DO
- ✅ Write minimal code to make test pass
- ✅ Use hardcoded values when appropriate
- ✅ Take small steps
- ✅ Verify all tests pass

### DON'T
- ❌ Implement beyond what tests demand
- ❌ Add features for future tests
- ❌ Optimize prematurely
- ❌ Refactor during Green phase

## Completion

After completing Green phase, proceed to Refactor phase:

```
🟢 Green Phase Complete. Proceeding to Refactor phase.
```

All 24 tests pass.

## Refactor Phase Summary

**Naming Evaluation**:
- All recent function/constant names (`reimbursedAmount`, `findItem`, `damagePayout`, `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_CLAIM_THRESHOLD`, `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_CLAIM_RATE`) clearly reveal intent. Kept as-is.

**Refactoring Applied**: Extracted `hasHighClaimEnchantment` predicate from `reimbursedAmount`.

**Before** (`reimbursedAmount`):
```ts
const reimbursedAmount = (damage: Damage, item: Item | undefined): number => {
  if (item && (item.enchantment ?? 0) >= HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_CLAIM_THRESHOLD) {
    return damage.amount * HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_CLAIM_RATE;
  }
  return damage.amount;
};
```
Mass: constants 1 + bindings 5 + invocations 1 + conditionals 8 (`if`, `&&`, `??`, `>=`) = ~15

**After**:
```ts
const hasHighClaimEnchantment = (item: Item | undefined): boolean =>
  (item?.enchantment ?? 0) >= HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_CLAIM_THRESHOLD;

const reimbursedAmount = (damage: Damage, item: Item | undefined): number =>
  hasHighClaimEnchantment(item) ? damage.amount * HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_CLAIM_RATE : damage.amount;
```
Combined mass: constants 1 + bindings 5 + invocations 2 + conditionals 8 (`?:`, `??`, `>=`, `?.`) = ~16

**Mass Change**: ~15 → ~16 (slight increase from extra invocation), but **Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) trumps APP**.

**Benefits**:
- The predicate `hasHighClaimEnchantment` clearly names the domain concept (Rule 2)
- Mirrors existing predicate pattern (`isComponent`, `isMainItem`) — consistency in the file
- Single-expression arrow function is more uniform with surrounding code
- Removes the awkward `item && (item.enchantment ?? 0)` null-check expression

**Tests**: All 24 passing.

**File**: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-18_19-55-22_claim-office-example-mapping_v6.3-no-pep_opus-4-7-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts`

# TDD Red Phase

You are now in the **Red Phase** of TDD. Follow these instructions to activate ONE test and make it fail.

## Your Mission

1. Activate exactly ONE test from the test list
2. Make explicit predictions about how it will fail
3. Verify the test fails for the right reason
4. NO implementation during Red phase

## Context: Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Activate: "dragon-material + high-enchantment: 50% rule wins (400 G on 1000 G damage)"
Current passing tests: 24
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts

This test exercises both clauses together. Per spec, 50% rule wins. With dragon material + enchantment >= 8, payout should be 1000*0.5 - 100 = 400.
Current code already produces this because high-enchantment branch is checked first and dragon material is not yet implemented. Expect: test passes as regression.

## Red Phase Rules

- **One test at a time**: Convert exactly ONE `it.todo()` to executable test
- **All other tests remain as `it.todo()`**: Never have more than one failing test
- **Two-stage failure**: First compilation error, then runtime/assertion error
- **Make predictions**: Explicitly state expected failures before running tests
- **No implementation**: Don't write code to make test pass yet

## Process

### Step 1: Activate One Test

Identify the next `it.todo()` and convert it to executable test code:

```typescript
// Convert from:
it.todo("should return 0 for empty input");

// To:
it("should return 0 for empty input", () => {
  expect(calculate("")).toBe(0);
});
```

Leave all other tests as `it.todo()`.

### Step 2: Predict Compilation Error

Before running the test, state your prediction:

```
🔴 Red Phase - Compilation Error Prediction:
- Test: "should return 0 for empty input"
- Expected: Compilation error
- Reason: Function `calculate` doesn't exist yet
- Error: "Cannot find name 'calculate'"
```

### Step 3: Run Test - Verify Compilation Error

Run `pnpm test:unit:basic` and verify:
- ✅ Compilation error as predicted, OR
- ❌ Prediction wrong → STOP and explain discrepancy

### Step 4: Create Empty Function

Create minimal function stub (no logic):

```typescript
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  return undefined as unknown as number;
};
```

### Step 5: Predict Runtime Error

Before running again, state your prediction:

```
🔴 Red Phase - Runtime Error Prediction:
- Test: "should return 0 for empty input"
- Expected: Runtime assertion error
- Expected value: 0
- Actual value: undefined
- Diff:
  Expected: 0
  Received: undefined
```

### Step 6: Run Test - Verify Runtime Error

Run `pnpm test:unit:basic` and verify:
- ✅ Assertion error as predicted, OR
- ❌ Prediction wrong → STOP and explain discrepancy

### Step 7: Report Completion

You MUST output the full Step 7 block verbatim with `Correct` or `Incorrect`
chosen for each prediction. Do not abbreviate. Do not collapse the two
prediction lines into one.

```
🔴 Red Phase Complete:
**Test Activated**: "should return 0 for empty input"
**Compilation Prediction**: Cannot find name 'calculate' ✅ Correct
**Runtime Prediction**: Expected 0, received undefined ✅ Correct
**Result**: Test fails as expected with assertion error

Proceeding to Green phase.
```

## Important Guidelines

### DO
- ✅ Activate exactly ONE test at a time
- ✅ Make explicit predictions before running tests
- ✅ Verify test fails for the right reason
- ✅ Keep all other tests as `it.todo()`

### DON'T
- ❌ Activate multiple tests
- ❌ Skip making predictions
- ❌ Write implementation to make test pass
- ❌ Continue if prediction fails without explanation

## Prediction Failure Protocol

If your prediction was wrong:

```
❌ Prediction Failed:
- Predicted: [what you expected]
- Actual: [what happened]
- Discrepancy: [explanation]

Investigating the discrepancy before proceeding.
```

## Completion

After completing Red phase, proceed to Green phase:

```
🔴 Red Phase Complete. Proceeding to Green phase.
```

Replaced `let amount = ...; if (...) amount -= ...; if (...) amount -= ...;` (3 assignments, mass 18) with a single pure expression. Now expresses the net rate as a sum/difference of discrete rate contributors.

2. **`itemModifierSurcharge`** — Extracted `curseRate`, `highEnchantmentRate`, and `itemModifierRate` helpers. Replaced `let surcharge = 0; if (...) surcharge += ...; if (...) surcharge += ...;` (3 assignments, mass 18) with a single ternary expression.

- `policyModifierAmount` before: ~47 (3 assignments + 2 conditionals + bindings/invocations)
- After (including helpers): ~38 (no assignments, ternaries replace assignment-conditionals, ~9 mass saved)
- `itemModifierSurcharge` before: ~38 (3 assignments + 3 conditionals + bindings/invocations)
- After (including helpers): ~30 (no assignments, ~8 mass saved)
- **Total mass reduction: ~17**

**Simple Design Rules Applied**:
- Rule 1 (Tests Pass): All 25 tests still passing
- Rule 2 (Reveals Intent): New helpers make the rate-based pricing structure explicit — "policy modifier is first-insurance minus loyalty minus follow-up"
- Rule 3 (No Duplication): Both functions had a similar "conditional rate accumulation" pattern, now both use the same functional style
- Rule 4 (Fewest Elements): Net code volume increased slightly, but mass decreased and the structure mirrors the domain (rate-based pricing)

**Tests**: All 25 passing.

## Your Mission

1. Activate exactly ONE test from the test list
2. Make explicit predictions about how it will fail

4. NO implementation during Red phase

## Context: Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Activate: "payout rounds down in MHPCO's favor"
Current passing tests: 26
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts

Need a scenario producing a fractional payout. With enchantment >=8, 50% can produce a fractional value. E.g. damage 901, enchantment 9: 901*0.5 = 450.5, -100 deductible = 350.5, floor = 350. Current code does Math.floor so it should pass.

## Red Phase Rules

- **One test at a time**: Convert exactly ONE `it.todo()` to executable test
- **All other tests remain as `it.todo()`**: Never have more than one failing test
- **Two-stage failure**: First compilation error, then runtime/assertion error
- **Make predictions**: Explicitly state expected failures before running tests
- **No implementation**: Don't write code to make test pass yet

## Process

### Step 1: Activate One Test

Identify the next `it.todo()` and convert it to executable test code:

```typescript
// Convert from:
it.todo("should return 0 for empty input");

// To:
it("should return 0 for empty input", () => {
  expect(calculate("")).toBe(0);
});
```

Leave all other tests as `it.todo()`.

### Step 2: Predict Compilation Error

Before running the test, state your prediction:

```
🔴 Red Phase - Compilation Error Prediction:
- Test: "should return 0 for empty input"
- Expected: Compilation error
- Reason: Function `calculate` doesn't exist yet
- Error: "Cannot find name 'calculate'"
```

### Step 3: Run Test - Verify Compilation Error

Run `pnpm test:unit:basic` and verify:
- ✅ Compilation error as predicted, OR
- ❌ Prediction wrong → STOP and explain discrepancy

### Step 4: Create Empty Function

Create minimal function stub (no logic):

```typescript
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  return undefined as unknown as number;
};
```

### Step 5: Predict Runtime Error

Before running again, state your prediction:

```
🔴 Red Phase - Runtime Error Prediction:
- Test: "should return 0 for empty input"
- Expected: Runtime assertion error
- Expected value: 0
- Actual value: undefined
- Diff:
  Expected: 0
  Received: undefined
```

### Step 6: Run Test - Verify Runtime Error

Run `pnpm test:unit:basic` and verify:
- ✅ Assertion error as predicted, OR
- ❌ Prediction wrong → STOP and explain discrepancy

### Step 7: Report Completion

You MUST output the full Step 7 block verbatim with `Correct` or `Incorrect`
chosen for each prediction. Do not abbreviate. Do not collapse the two
prediction lines into one.

```
🔴 Red Phase Complete:
**Test Activated**: "should return 0 for empty input"
**Compilation Prediction**: Cannot find name 'calculate' ✅ Correct
**Runtime Prediction**: Expected 0, received undefined ✅ Correct
**Result**: Test fails as expected with assertion error

Proceeding to Green phase.
```

## Important Guidelines

### DO
- ✅ Activate exactly ONE test at a time
- ✅ Make explicit predictions before running tests
- ✅ Verify test fails for the right reason
- ✅ Keep all other tests as `it.todo()`

### DON'T
- ❌ Activate multiple tests
- ❌ Skip making predictions
- ❌ Write implementation to make test pass
- ❌ Continue if prediction fails without explanation

## Prediction Failure Protocol

If your prediction was wrong:

```
❌ Prediction Failed:
- Predicted: [what you expected]
- Actual: [what happened]
- Discrepancy: [explanation]

Investigating the discrepancy before proceeding.
```

## Completion

After completing Red phase, proceed to Green phase:

```
🔴 Red Phase Complete. Proceeding to Green phase.
```

Per spec: two swords insured. Damages [{sword, 500}, {sword, 600}] → payout = (500-100)+(600-100) = 900. But also spec mentions "if damages array contains more entries of a given type than the policy actually covers (e.g. two sword damages but only one sword insured) → CLI exits with a non-zero status; whole claim is rejected". So this test verifies 2 swords + 2 damages is OK and each has own deductible. Current code: damagePayout loops over damages independently. So 900 payout, cap = 2*2000 = 4000, remaining 3100. Expect: passes as regression.

## Your Mission

## Green Phase Rules

- **No refactoring during Green phase**: Save improvements for Refactor phase

## Process

### Step 1: Analyze the Failing Test

Identify:
- What input does the test provide?
- What output does it expect?
- What is the simplest way to produce that output?

### Step 2: Write Minimal Implementation

Implement only what's needed to make the current test pass:

```typescript
// For first test "should return 0 for empty input":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  return 0;
};

// For second test "should return number for single input":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  if (input === "") return 0;
  return parseInt(input);
};

// For third test "should add two numbers":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  if (input === "") return 0;
  const numbers = input.split(",");
  if (numbers.length === 1) return parseInt(numbers[0]);
  return parseInt(numbers[0]) + parseInt(numbers[1]);
};
```

### Step 3: Run Tests

Run `pnpm test:unit:basic` and verify:
- Current test now passes ✅

### Step 4: Verify No Over-Implementation

Check:

- ❓ Did I add logic not demanded by current test? → Remove it
- ❓ Did I optimize prematurely? → Simplify
- ❓ Did I refactor existing code? → Revert, save for Refactor phase

### Step 5: Report Completion



## Important Guidelines

- ✅ Verify all tests pass

### DON'T

You are now in the **Red Phase** of TDD. Follow these instructions to activate ONE test and make it fail.

## Your Mission

1. Activate exactly ONE test from the test list
2. Make explicit predictions about how it will fail
3. Verify the test fails for the right reason
4. NO implementation during Red phase

## Context: Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Activate: "claim damage references item not in policy → CLI exits non-zero"
Current passing tests: 30
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts

This needs claim validation. If damage.itemType is not in policy.items types, throw. Currently findItem returns undefined and damagePayout uses Math.max(0, amount - 100). So an amulet damage on a sword policy would silently pay (amount-100). Need to throw.

Test: quote sword, claim with damage on "amulet" → throws.

## Red Phase Rules

- **One test at a time**: Convert exactly ONE `it.todo()` to executable test
- **All other tests remain as `it.todo()`**: Never have more than one failing test
- **Two-stage failure**: First compilation error, then runtime/assertion error
- **Make predictions**: Explicitly state expected failures before running tests
- **No implementation**: Don't write code to make test pass yet

## Process

### Step 1: Activate One Test

Identify the next `it.todo()` and convert it to executable test code:

```typescript
// Convert from:
it.todo("should return 0 for empty input");

// To:
it("should return 0 for empty input", () => {
  expect(calculate("")).toBe(0);
});
```

Leave all other tests as `it.todo()`.

### Step 2: Predict Compilation Error

Before running the test, state your prediction:

```
🔴 Red Phase - Compilation Error Prediction:
- Test: "should return 0 for empty input"
- Expected: Compilation error
- Reason: Function `calculate` doesn't exist yet
- Error: "Cannot find name 'calculate'"
```

### Step 3: Run Test - Verify Compilation Error

Run `pnpm test:unit:basic` and verify:
- ✅ Compilation error as predicted, OR
- ❌ Prediction wrong → STOP and explain discrepancy

### Step 4: Create Empty Function

Create minimal function stub (no logic):

```typescript
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  return undefined as unknown as number;
};
```

### Step 5: Predict Runtime Error

Before running again, state your prediction:

```
🔴 Red Phase - Runtime Error Prediction:
- Test: "should return 0 for empty input"
- Expected: Runtime assertion error
- Expected value: 0
- Actual value: undefined
- Diff:
  Expected: 0
  Received: undefined
```

### Step 6: Run Test - Verify Runtime Error

Run `pnpm test:unit:basic` and verify:
- ✅ Assertion error as predicted, OR
- ❌ Prediction wrong → STOP and explain discrepancy

### Step 7: Report Completion

You MUST output the full Step 7 block verbatim with `Correct` or `Incorrect`
chosen for each prediction. Do not abbreviate. Do not collapse the two
prediction lines into one.

```
🔴 Red Phase Complete:
**Test Activated**: "should return 0 for empty input"
**Compilation Prediction**: Cannot find name 'calculate' ✅ Correct
**Runtime Prediction**: Expected 0, received undefined ✅ Correct
**Result**: Test fails as expected with assertion error

Proceeding to Green phase.
```

## Important Guidelines

### DO
- ✅ Activate exactly ONE test at a time
- ✅ Make explicit predictions before running tests
- ✅ Verify test fails for the right reason
- ✅ Keep all other tests as `it.todo()`

### DON'T
- ❌ Activate multiple tests
- ❌ Skip making predictions
- ❌ Write implementation to make test pass
- ❌ Continue if prediction fails without explanation

## Prediction Failure Protocol

If your prediction was wrong:

```
❌ Prediction Failed:
- Predicted: [what you expected]
- Actual: [what happened]
- Discrepancy: [explanation]

Investigating the discrepancy before proceeding.
```

After completing Red phase, proceed to Green phase:

```
🔴 Red Phase Complete. Proceeding to Green phase.
```

## Refactor Phase Summary

**Test file:** `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-18_19-55-22_claim-office-example-mapping_v6.3-no-pep_opus-4-7-no-thinking/src/claim-office.spec.ts`

### Naming Evaluation (First Priority)
- `validateClaimDamages` — clearly reveals intent. Kept.
- `validateQuoteItems` — clearly reveals intent. Kept.
- Renamed parameter `i` to `item` for clarity (Rule 2 reveals intent).
- New helper `assertAllValid` — name reveals the assertion semantics (throws on failure).
- New helper `insuredTypesOf` — descriptive, parallels `KNOWN_ITEM_TYPES` set.

### Refactoring Applied

**Rule 3 (No Duplication):** Both validators followed an identical pattern: build set of valid identifiers, find first invalid element, throw with custom message. Extracted shared helper `assertAllValid<T>(collection, isValid, buildError)` parameterized by predicate and error builder. This consolidates the validation pattern as genuine knowledge ("throw on first invalid element") rather than incidental code similarity.

**Rule 2 (Reveals Intent):** Extracted `insuredTypesOf(items)` to give a name to the operation of projecting policy items into their set of insured types. Made the structural symmetry between the two validators explicit: each is a one-line declaration showing collection, validity predicate, and error message.

### APP Mass Calculation

**Before (validators combined):**
- `validateQuoteItems`: constants=1, bindings=2, invocations=3, conditionals=2, loops=1, assignments=0 = 1+2+6+8+5+0 = 22
- `validateClaimDamages`: constants=1, bindings=5, invocations=5, conditionals=2, loops=2, assignments=0 = 1+5+10+8+10+0 = 34
- **Combined Total: ~56**

**After (validators + extracted helper):**
- `assertAllValid`: constants=0, bindings=4, invocations=3, conditionals=2, loops=1, assignments=0 = 0+4+6+8+5+0 = 23
- `validateQuoteItems`: bindings=1, invocations=2 = 1+4 = 5
- `insuredTypesOf`: bindings=2, invocations=2, loops=1 = 2+4+5 = 11
- `validateClaimDamages`: bindings=3, invocations=3, assignments=0 = 3+6 = 9
- **Combined Total: ~48**

content.text:
# TDD Red Phase

You are now in the **Red Phase** of TDD. Follow these instructions to activate ONE test and make it fail.

## Your Mission

1. Activate exactly ONE test from the test list
2. Make explicit predictions about how it will fail
3. Verify the test fails for the right reason
4. NO implementation during Red phase

## Context: Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Activate: "damage entries exceeding insured count → CLI exits non-zero"
Current passing tests: 31
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts

Per spec: "if the damages array contains more entries of a given type than the policy actually covers (e.g. two sword damages but only one sword insured) → CLI exits with non-zero status; whole claim is rejected". Current validation only checks if any damage references a type not in the policy, not the count. So 2 sword damages on a 1-sword policy passes validation silently.

Test: quote 1 sword, claim with 2 sword damages → throws.

## Red Phase Rules

- **One test at a time**: Convert exactly ONE `it.todo()` to executable test
- **All other tests remain as `it.todo()`**: Never have more than one failing test
- **Two-stage failure**: First compilation error, then runtime/assertion error
- **Make predictions**: Explicitly state expected failures before running tests
- **No implementation**: Don't write code to make test pass yet

## Process

### Step 1: Activate One Test

Identify the next `it.todo()` and convert it to executable test code:

```typescript
// Convert from:
it.todo("should return 0 for empty input");

// To:
it("should return 0 for empty input", () => {
  expect(calculate("")).toBe(0);
});
```

Leave all other tests as `it.todo()`.

### Step 2: Predict Compilation Error

Before running the test, state your prediction:

```
🔴 Red Phase - Compilation Error Prediction:
- Test: "should return 0 for empty input"
- Expected: Compilation error
- Reason: Function `calculate` doesn't exist yet
- Error: "Cannot find name 'calculate'"
```

### Step 3: Run Test - Verify Compilation Error

Run `pnpm test:unit:basic` and verify:
- ✅ Compilation error as predicted, OR
- ❌ Prediction wrong → STOP and explain discrepancy

### Step 4: Create Empty Function

Create minimal function stub (no logic):

```typescript
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  return undefined as unknown as number;
};
```

### Step 5: Predict Runtime Error

Before running again, state your prediction:

```
🔴 Red Phase - Runtime Error Prediction:
- Test: "should return 0 for empty input"
- Expected: Runtime assertion error
- Expected value: 0
- Actual value: undefined
- Diff:
  Expected: 0
  Received: undefined
```

### Step 6: Run Test - Verify Runtime Error

Run `pnpm test:unit:basic` and verify:
- ✅ Assertion error as predicted, OR
- ❌ Prediction wrong → STOP and explain discrepancy

### Step 7: Report Completion

You MUST output the full Step 7 block verbatim with `Correct` or `Incorrect`
chosen for each prediction. Do not abbreviate. Do not collapse the two
prediction lines into one.

```
🔴 Red Phase Complete:
**Test Activated**: "should return 0 for empty input"
**Compilation Prediction**: Cannot find name 'calculate' ✅ Correct
**Runtime Prediction**: Expected 0, received undefined ✅ Correct
**Result**: Test fails as expected with assertion error

Proceeding to Green phase.
```

## Important Guidelines

### DO
- ✅ Activate exactly ONE test at a time
- ✅ Make explicit predictions before running tests
- ✅ Verify test fails for the right reason
- ✅ Keep all other tests as `it.todo()`

### DON'T
- ❌ Activate multiple tests
- ❌ Skip making predictions
- ❌ Write implementation to make test pass
- ❌ Continue if prediction fails without explanation

## Prediction Failure Protocol

If your prediction was wrong:

```
❌ Prediction Failed:
- Predicted: [what you expected]
- Actual: [what happened]
- Discrepancy: [explanation]

Investigating the discrepancy before proceeding.
```

## Completion

After completing Red phase, proceed to Green phase:

```
🔴 Red Phase Complete. Proceeding to Green phase.
```

content.text:
# TDD Green Phase

You are now in the **Green Phase** of TDD. Follow these instructions to make the failing test pass with MINIMAL code.

## Your Mission

1. Implement the minimal code necessary to make the failing test pass
2. Use the simplest possible solution (hardcoded values are acceptable)
3. Do not add features for future tests
4. Verify all tests pass
5. No optimization, no refactoring during Green phase

## Context: Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Failing test: "damage entries exceeding insured count → CLI exits non-zero"
Current error: function did not throw
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts

Need to extend validateClaimDamages to also count damage entries per type and throw if count > insured count of that type. Use countsByType for both damages (by itemType) and policy items (by type).

## Green Phase Rules

- **Minimal code only**: Just enough to pass the current test
- **Smallest change**: Make the smallest possible change
- **No future features**: Don't implement what future tests might need
- **Hardcoded returns are allowed**
- **Tests must pass**: Verify all tests are green
- **No refactoring during Green phase**: Save improvements for Refactor phase

## Process

### Step 1: Analyze the Failing Test

Identify:
- What input does the test provide?
- What output does it expect?
- What is the simplest way to produce that output?

### Step 2: Write Minimal Implementation

Implement only what's needed to make the current test pass:

```typescript
// For first test "should return 0 for empty input":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  return 0;
};

// For second test "should return number for single input":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  if (input === "") return 0;
  return parseInt(input);
};

// For third test "should add two numbers":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  if (input === "") return 0;
  const numbers = input.split(",");
  if (numbers.length === 1) return parseInt(numbers[0]);
  return parseInt(numbers[0]) + parseInt(numbers[1]);
};
```

### Step 3: Run Tests

Run `pnpm test:unit:basic` and verify:
- Current test now passes ✅
- All previous tests still pass ✅

### Step 4: Verify No Over-Implementation

Check:
- ❓ Did I implement features for future tests? → Remove them
- ❓ Did I add logic not demanded by current test? → Remove it
- ❓ Did I optimize prematurely? → Simplify
- ❓ Did I refactor existing code? → Revert, save for Refactor phase

### Step 5: Report Completion

```
🟢 Green Phase Complete:
**Implementation**: [brief description of what was added]
**Result**: All tests now pass ([X] passing)

Proceeding to Refactor phase.
```

## Minimal Implementation Strategies

### Hardcoded Returns

```typescript
// Test: "should return 0 for empty input"
return 0;
```

### Simple Conditionals

```typescript
// Test: "should return number for single input"
if (input === "") return 0;
return parseInt(input);
```

### Generalization (only when forced by a new test)

```typescript
// Test: "should add multiple numbers"
return input.split(",").reduce((sum, n) => sum + parseInt(n), 0);
```

## Important Guidelines

### DO
- ✅ Write minimal code to make test pass
- ✅ Use hardcoded values when appropriate
- ✅ Take small steps
- ✅ Verify all tests pass

### DON'T
- ❌ Implement beyond what tests demand
- ❌ Add features for future tests
- ❌ Optimize prematurely
- ❌ Refactor during Green phase

## Completion

After completing Green phase, proceed to Refactor phase:

```
🟢 Green Phase Complete. Proceeding to Refactor phase.
```

The refactoring is complete and tests pass. Let me summarize the refactoring decisions.

## Refactor Phase Summary

**Naming Evaluation (First Priority)**:
- `validateClaimDamages` — Clear and accurate. Reveals the intent (validating damage entries against the policy). Keep.
- `countsByType` — Clear and accurate. Keep.
- Introduced `countsBy` as a generic helper — name reveals intent (counts items grouped by a key derived by `keyOf`).

**Refactoring Applied** (Rule 3: No Duplication, then Rule 2: Reveals Intent):

1. **Extracted generic `countsBy<T>(items, keyOf)` helper**: The recent green phase reimplemented the counting-by-key pattern imperatively for `Damage[]`, duplicating the conceptual logic in `countsByType(Item[])`. Both now share the underlying `countsBy` helper:
   - `countsByType(items)` becomes `countsBy(items, (item) => item.type)`
   - New: `countsBy(damages, (damage) => damage.itemType)`

2. **Replaced imperative `for`-loops with declarative calls**: The new code uses `countsBy` (reduce-based) and the existing `assertAllValid` helper for the count-comparison check. This:
   - Removes conceptual duplication with the validation pattern already established (`assertAllValid` is reused for the second validation, consistent with the first one in the same function)
   - Eliminates two `for...of` loops and a `throw` statement

**APP Mass — Before** (recent green phase block, lines 217–226):
- Constants: ~3 (`1`, `0`, error string template) = 3
- Bindings: ~9 (insuredCounts, damageCounts, damage, itemType, count, etc.) = 9
- Invocations: ~9 (`countsByType`, `new Map`, `set`, `get`, `??` x2, etc.) = 18
- Conditionals: 3 (`??` x2, `if`) = 12
- Loops: 2 (`for...of` x2) = 10
- Assignments: 0
- **Subtotal**: ~52

**APP Mass — After** (lines 221–227):
- Constants: ~1 (error string template) = 1
- Bindings: ~5 (insuredCounts, damageCounts, damage, itemType, count) = 5
- Invocations: ~6 (`countsByType`, `countsBy`, `assertAllValid`, `Array.from`, `entries`, `get`, `??`) = 12
- Conditionals: 2 (`??`, `<=`) = 8
- Loops: 0 (loops now encapsulated in helpers) = 0
- Assignments: 0
- **Subtotal**: ~26

Plus the generic `countsBy` adds ~10 mass, but `countsByType` shrinks since it now delegates. Net effect: **mass reduced and conceptual duplication eliminated**.

**Rule check**:
- Rule 1 (Tests pass): 32/32 passing
- Rule 2 (Reveals intent): Improved — validation reads as "all damage-count entries satisfy `count <= insured`"
- Rule 3 (No duplication): Improved — counting-by-key pattern unified into `countsBy`
- Rule 4 (Fewest elements): Net reduction in elements at the call site; one new tiny generic helper added (justified by reuse)

content.text:
# TDD Green Phase

You are now in the **Green Phase** of TDD. Follow these instructions to make the failing test pass with MINIMAL code.

## Your Mission

1. Implement the minimal code necessary to make the failing test pass
2. Use the simplest possible solution (hardcoded values are acceptable)
3. Do not add features for future tests
4. Verify all tests pass
5. No optimization, no refactoring during Green phase

## Context: Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Failing test: "negative damage amount → CLI exits non-zero"
Current error: function did not throw on negative damage amount
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts

Need to validate damage.amount >= 0 in validateClaimDamages using assertAllValid.

## Green Phase Rules

- **Minimal code only**: Just enough to pass the current test
- **Smallest change**: Make the smallest possible change
- **No future features**: Don't implement what future tests might need
- **Hardcoded returns are allowed**
- **Tests must pass**: Verify all tests are green
- **No refactoring during Green phase**: Save improvements for Refactor phase

## Process

### Step 1: Analyze the Failing Test

Identify:
- What input does the test provide?
- What output does it expect?
- What is the simplest way to produce that output?

### Step 2: Write Minimal Implementation

Implement only what's needed to make the current test pass:

```typescript
// For first test "should return 0 for empty input":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  return 0;
};

// For second test "should return number for single input":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  if (input === "") return 0;
  return parseInt(input);
};

// For third test "should add two numbers":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  if (input === "") return 0;
  const numbers = input.split(",");
  if (numbers.length === 1) return parseInt(numbers[0]);
  return parseInt(numbers[0]) + parseInt(numbers[1]);
};
```

### Step 3: Run Tests

Run `pnpm test:unit:basic` and verify:
- Current test now passes ✅
- All previous tests still pass ✅

### Step 4: Verify No Over-Implementation

Check:
- ❓ Did I implement features for future tests? → Remove them
- ❓ Did I add logic not demanded by current test? → Remove it
- ❓ Did I optimize prematurely? → Simplify
- ❓ Did I refactor existing code? → Revert, save for Refactor phase

### Step 5: Report Completion

```
🟢 Green Phase Complete:
**Implementation**: [brief description of what was added]
**Result**: All tests now pass ([X] passing)

Proceeding to Refactor phase.
```

## Minimal Implementation Strategies

### Hardcoded Returns

```typescript
// Test: "should return 0 for empty input"
return 0;
```

### Simple Conditionals

```typescript
// Test: "should return number for single input"
if (input === "") return 0;
return parseInt(input);
```

### Generalization (only when forced by a new test)

```typescript
// Test: "should add multiple numbers"
return input.split(",").reduce((sum, n) => sum + parseInt(n), 0);
```

## Important Guidelines

### DO
- ✅ Write minimal code to make test pass
- ✅ Use hardcoded values when appropriate
- ✅ Take small steps
- ✅ Verify all tests pass

### DON'T
- ❌ Implement beyond what tests demand
- ❌ Add features for future tests
- ❌ Optimize prematurely
- ❌ Refactor during Green phase

## Completion

After completing Green phase, proceed to Refactor phase:

```
🟢 Green Phase Complete. Proceeding to Refactor phase.
```

## Refactor Phase Summary

### Naming Evaluation
- `validateClaimDamages`: name still accurately captures intent — kept.
- The three inline validation lambdas inside `validateClaimDamages` lacked named intent — extracted as named helpers.

### Refactoring Applied (Rule 2: Reveals Intent)

Extracted three named validation helpers from `validateClaimDamages`:
- `assertNonNegativeDamageAmounts(damages)`
- `assertDamagesReferenceInsuredItems(damages, policyItems)`
- `assertDamageCountsWithinInsured(damages, policyItems)`

`validateClaimDamages` now reads as three clearly-named domain assertions instead of a dense block of three `assertAllValid` calls with inline lambdas.

### APP Mass Comparison

**Before (`validateClaimDamages` body)**:
- Bindings (insuredTypes, insuredCounts, damageCounts, lambdas params): ~10
- Invocations (3x assertAllValid + insuredTypesOf, countsByType, countsBy, Array.from, .entries, .has, .get, comparisons, template literals): ~22 → ×2 = ~44
- Conditionals (`?? 0`): 1 → ×4 = 4
- Approx total: ~58

**After (across 4 functions)**:
- The mass is roughly equivalent (the operations are the same), but distributed across four small, single-purpose functions.
- The orchestrator `validateClaimDamages` has mass ~6 (3 invocations × 2). Each helper holds one assertion's mass locally.

Mass is essentially conserved (Rule 2 takes precedence over APP per the priority rules) but **intent is significantly clearer** — each validation now has a self-documenting name visible at the call site.

### Rule Application
- Rule 1 (Tests Pass): All 33 tests green.
- Rule 2 (Reveals Intent): Improved — three named, single-purpose validation functions.
- Rule 3 (No Duplication): No duplication present (already using shared `assertAllValid`).
- Rule 4 (Fewest Elements): Three new function bindings added, accepted as a trade for clarity (Rule 2 trumps Rule 4).
